D Background
D
1. In these proceedings, the Plaintiff (“LY”) applied to set aside
E
E
a final arbitral award on merits and quantum dated 21 October 2021
F
(“Award”) made by a tribunal of 3 arbitrators (“Tribunal”) in an F
arbitration which had been commenced by the Defendant (“HW”) in
G
G
Hong Kong against LY (“Arbitration”). The grounds relied upon for the
H
setting aside are that the arbitral procedure was not in accordance with the H
parties’ agreement, in that the Tribunal failed to deal with all the key issues
I
I
which had been put before it, and/or the Tribunal had failed to provide
J
sufficient reasons for its decisions on the key issues; and that the Award is J
in conflict with the public policy of Hong Kong.
K
K
L
2. HW opposed the application, firstly on the ground that a L
failure to deal with key issues cannot give rise to an order setting aside the
M
M
Award under section 81 of the Arbitration Ordinance (“Ordinance”).
N
Reliance was placed on the decision in Brunswick Bowling & Biliards N
O Corp v Shanghai Zhonglu Industrial Co Ltd [2011] 1 HKLRD, where the
O
Court pointed out that a tribunal’s failure to consider an issue is a matter
P
P
which goes to the substantive decision rather than a failure to follow the
Q arbitral procedure agreed by the parties. It is at most an error of law which
Q
cannot be a basis for the Court to set aside the award.
R
R
S 3. On behalf of HW, Mr Manzoni SC also referred to the travaux
S
preparatoires to Article 34 of the Model Law (“Article 34”), which
T
T
suggested that the drafters had considered the possibility of adding a
U
U
V
V

- 3 -
A A
separate ground of infra petita for setting aside, but it was not eventually
B
B
adopted. However, as Mr Yu SC pointed out on behalf of LY, the Working
C Group was then considering the possibility of adding infra petita as a
C
specific ground for setting aside, and the eventual decision of the Working
D
D
Group was to limit the scope of Article 34 to the grounds specified in the
E
New York Convention, to ensure a high degree of consistency with the E
Convention. The drafters of Article 34 considered that a claim or
F
F
complaint of infra petita would in any event fall within the ground of
G
public policy already included in Article 34. The authorities show that G
once infra petita is made out, it would be a valid ground of challenge, as
H
H
an arbitral procedure which is in contravention of principles of natural
I
justice and basic standards of fairness would not be a procedure to which I
parties to an arbitration agreement would have agreed. The authorities
J
J
referred to by Counsel include Joseph, Jurisdiction and Arbitration
K
Agreements And Their Enforcement (3rd ed), para 16.42- 16.43; Binder, K
International Commercial Arbitration and Mediation in UNCITRAL Model
L
L
Law Jurisdictions (2019), p 448; Born, International Commercial
M
Arbitration (3rd ed) pp 3899-3900; and Waincymer, Procedure and M
Evidence in International Arbitration (2012) para 16.19.1. Counsel also
N
N
referred to this Court’s earlier decisions in Z v R unreported,
O
O
HCCT 11/2021, 9 August 2021 and N v C unreported, HCCT 3/2019,
P 16 September 2019.
P
Q
Q
4. N v C was a case decided on the ground of serious irregularity,
R under section 4 of Schedule 2 of the Ordinance, which expressly includes
R
as a ground for challenging an arbitral award a failure by the tribunal to
S
S
deal with all the issues that were put to it, which has caused substantial
T injustice to the applicant.
T
U
U
V
V

- 4 -
A A
5. As for the judgment in Brunswick, I would agree that it is
B
B
generally correct to say that a tribunal’s failure to consider an issue would
C normally be a matter which goes to the substantive decision, which may
C
amount only to an error of law, which is not a ground for challenging the
D
D
award. It remains to be seen whether the tribunal has failed to actually
E
decide or deal with an issue, what the issue is, and in the context of the E
present dispute, whether the tribunal failed to give reasons for its decision
F
F
on an issue claimed to be essential to the determination of the dispute
G
submitted to the tribunal, and whether such failure can constitute a failure G
to follow the parties’ agreed arbitral procedure, to become a ground under
H
H
section 81 of the Ordinance for setting aside.
I
I
6. In A v B [2015] 3 HKLRD 586, for example, the tribunal’s
J
J
failure to deal with the important defence, of the claim being time-barred,
K
was considered to be not just an error of law made, but a denial of due K
process causing substantial injustice and unfairness to the parties, as the
L
L
parties were entitled to expect the limitation defence, which would have
M
defeated any and all claims raised, to be addressed and to be seen from the M
award to have been considered by the tribunal. It was most evidently a key
N
N
and critical issue, which was not considered or dealt with by the tribunal at
O
O
all.
P
P
7. In the present case, however, the arguments made by Counsel
Q
Q
on this preliminary point on Article 34(2)(a)(iii) make no difference of
R significance, since LY has applied under both Article 34 (2) (a) (iii) and
R
Article 34 (2) (b) (ii) to set aside the Award. Even if the Tribunal’s alleged
S
S
failure to deal with a key issue does not come within the ground of non-
T compliance with the agreed arbitral procedure under Article 34 (2) (a) (iii),
T
U
U
V
V

- 5 -
A A
LY is still entitled to claim that such failure is against the public policy of
B
B
Hong Kong as a matter undermining due process, and being contrary to
C basic notions of justice and fairness, to invoke the Court’s discretion to set
C
aside the Award as a result. That was the approach adopted by this Court
D
D
for the setting aside in A v B [2015] 3 HKLRD 586.
E
E
8. On the general question of whether or not a ground has been
F
F
established to seek setting aside as the exclusive recourse against an award
G
under Article 34, given effect by section 81 of the Ordinance, the G
authorities are clear. In Grand Pacific Holdings Ltd v Pacific China
H
H
Holdings Ltd (in liq) (No 1) [2012] 4 HKLRD 1, the Court referred to the
I
commentary of Professor van den Berg in “The New York Convention of I
1958: An Overview”, that “the grounds for refusal of enforcement are to be
J
J
construed narrowly”, which means that “their existence is accepted in
K
serious cases only”, and that “the courts appear to accept a violation of due K
process in serious cases only, thereby applying the general rule of
L
L
interpretation of article V of the New York Convention that the grounds
M
for refusal of enforcement are to be construed narrowly”. At paragraph 94 M
of the judgment of Tang VP (as His Lordship then was), it was observed:
N
N
“I am inclined to the view that the conduct complained of must
O
be sufficiently serious or egregious so that one could say a party O
has been denied due process.”
P
P
9. A similarly narrow approach has been adopted by the courts to
Q
Q
the construction of the “public policy” ground for setting aside. In
R
Qinhuangdao Tongda Enterprise Development Co v Million Basic Co Ltd R
[1993] 1 HKLR 173, 178, the Court emphasized that the “public policy”
S
S
ground must not be seen as a catch-all provision to be used wherever
T
convenient. It is limited in scope and is to be sparingly applied. “Contrary T
U
U
V
V

- 6 -
A A
to public policy” has been held by the Court of Final Appeal to mean
B
B
“contrary to the fundamental conceptions of morality and justice” of the
C forum (Hebei Import & Export Corp v Polytek Engineering Co Ltd
C
(1999) 2 HKCFAR 111, 139F). If the public policy ground is to be raised,
D
D
“there must be something more, that is, a substantial injustice arising out of
E
an award which is so shocking to the court’s conscience as to render E
enforcement repugnant” (A v R (Arbitration: Enforcement) [2009] 3
F
F
HKLRD 389).
G
G
H The dispute in the Arbitration
H
10. LY is a company incorporated in Hong Kong, and HW is a
I
I
company incorporated on the Mainland. Both are in the business of
J
pharmaceutical distribution on the Mainland. J
K
K
11. HW entered into a Distribution Agreement dated 29 January
L
2015 (“Agreement”), under which HW was appointed the exclusive L
distributor of S products (“Products”) of XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
M
M
(“AZ”), a company also engaged in the business of manufacturing and
N
distributing pharmaceutical products. AZ is an affiliated company of N
O XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX, whereas HW is a member of the group of
O
companies forming part of XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX, a publicly listed
P
P
company in Hong Kong.
Q
Q
12. Under the Agreement, HW was to purchase the Products in
R
R
its own name from AZ, and sell them in its own name within the Mainland,
S subject to and in accordance with the terms set out in the Agreement.
S
T
T
U
U
V
V

- 7 -
A A
13. By a notice dated 27 June 2018 (“Notice”), AZ assigned to
B
B
LY all its rights and obligations under the Agreement, with effect from
C 28 June 2018 (“Assignment”). The validity of the Assignment is not
C
disputed in the Arbitration. By virtue of the Assignment, LY stepped into
D
D
the shoes of AZ, and HW became LY’s exclusive distributor of the
E
Products on the Mainland. Further, it is not disputed that Schedule 1 of the E
Notice referred to a letter dated 1 August 2016 (“2016 Letter”), from
F
F
HW’s parent company and accepted by AZ’s parent company, as a
G
supplemental agreement between HW and AZ, and that both HW and AZ G
agreed to be bound by the 2016 Letter. The contents of the 2016 Letter will
H
H
be dealt with below.
I
I
14. On 17 May 2019, LY issued a notice of termination of the
J
J
Agreement, on the ground that HW had failed to achieve the annual growth
K
rate target agreed, in that the annual sales value (“ASV”) for 2018 was K
merely RMB 353.31 million, which is lower than RMB 354 million as the
L
L
ASV required under the 2016 Letter. The purported termination was
M
disputed by HW, and on 29 July 2019, HW filed a notice of arbitration in M
accordance with the dispute resolution mechanism prescribed under the
N
N
Agreement.
O
O
P 15. In the Arbitration, HW sought (1) a declaration that LY was in
P
breach of the Agreement, and that its purported termination is invalid;
Q
Q
(2) an order that LY’s invalid termination had been accepted by HW; and
R (3) damages for breach, with interest and costs. HW put forward various
R
contentions in relation to the calculation of the ASV figure, and one of
S
S
such contentions was that HW had met the ASV target for 2018, as the
T 2017 “rollover” sales volumes should have been included in the calculation
T
U
U
V
V

- 8 -
A A
of the 2018 ASV figure, in accordance with a Rollover Arrangement
B
B
agreed, by virtue of which the sales value representing inventory volume in
C excess of certain inventory days should be deducted from the current
C
year’s figures (i.e. 2017) and included in the next year’s figures (i.e. for
D
D
2018) (“Rollover Arrangement”).
E
E
16. On its part, LY put forward and relied on its calculation of the
F
F
ASV for 2018 being RMB 353.31 million.
G
G
17. Hence, the parties were in dispute over how the ASV should
H
H
be calculated under the Agreement, and whether the ASV target of
I
RMB 354 million for 2018 had been met. If LY’s calculation of the ASV I
for 2018 was not accepted by the Tribunal, the purported termination of the
J
J
Agreement in May 2019 would have been wrongful, LY would have been
K
in breach of the Agreement, and liable for the damages sought by HW. K
L
L
18. The above is reflected in the pleadings served in the
M
Arbitration, by the parties’ submissions, and correctly pointed out by the M
Tribunal, at paragraphs 64 and 73 of the Award:
N
N
“64. The parties are agreed that the sales in 2017 exceeded
O
RMB 290m. The parties disagree over whether the target of O
RMB 354m for 2018 was met.
P
… P
Q 73. At the heart of this arbitration is the parties’ disagreement
Q
over the correct calculation of the annual sales value. (HW)’s
case is that the target for 2018 was met in one or more ways
R
explained below.” (Emphases added) R
S
S
T
T
U
U
V
V

- 9 -
A A
The issues not dealt with
B
B
19. According to LY, the Tribunal failed in its Award to deal with
C
3 issues which had been expressly drawn to the attention of the Tribunal, C
D which issues were integral to the resolution of the parties’ dispute
D
concerning the Rollover Arrangement relied upon by HW for its
E
E
calculation of the ASV under the Agreement. The Rollover Arrangement
F had been put forward by HW as an arrangement which had been made and
F
agreed between HW and AZ, whereby sales value representing inventory
G
G
volume in excess of certain inventory days should be deducted from the
H figure of a particular year, and included in the next year.
H
I
I
20. LY claims that in the Arbitration, the parties had disputed
J whether the alleged Rollover Arrangement existed, and if it existed,
J
whether it would be consistent with the Agreement, in light of the
K
K
definition of ASV in section 10.2 of the Agreement, and the need for any
L amendment to the Agreement to be signed and in writing in accordance
L
with section 38.1. This was referred to as the “sections 10.2 and 38.1
M
M
Issue”.
N
N
21. The second disputed issue which had been put to the Tribunal,
O
O
on LY’s case, was whether the Joint Review Committee established under
P
section 13.1 of the Agreement (“JRC”) had power to make a P
determination on the ASV for future years, and whether the determination
Q
Q
by the JRC recorded in the minutes of a meeting of the JRC held on
R
18 January 2016 (“2016 Minutes”), to determine the inventory days at the R
end of 2015, and that the portion exceeding 55 days would not be included
S
S
in the calculation of rebate for 2015 and would be included in the
T
2016 sales value for the calculation of rebate (“JRC Agreement”), had T
U
U
V
V

- 10 -
A A
effect on the parties’ rights and obligations under the Agreement, and
B
B
whether the JRC Agreement amounted to a valid amendment of the
C Agreement by virtue of section 38.1. This was referred to as the
C
“sections 13.3 and 38.1 Issue”.
D
D
E
22. Finally, LY claims that the third issue in dispute was whether E
the Rollover Arrangement would be binding on LY as an assignee under
F
F
Article 82 of the PRC Contract Law, given LY’s state of knowledge (or
G
lack of knowledge) of the practice between HW and AZ. This was G
referred to as the “Assignment Issue”.
H
H
I
23. It was emphasized on behalf of LY that if any of these 3 issues I
had been found in favour of LY in the Arbitration, the sales value in 2017
J
J
could not have been carried over and taken into account for the calculation
K
of the ASV for 2018, such that HW would have been in breach of K
agreement for the 2018 target, and LY would have been entitled to
L
L
terminate the Agreement.
M
M
24. It was accordingly claimed that as evident from the Award, the
N
N
Tribunal had failed to deal with key issues put before it, and that the
O
O
reasons provided by the Tribunal in the Award were insufficient to enable
P the parties to understand the legal basis on which the Tribunal had found
P
against LY on the Rollover Arrangement issue, which amounted to a
Q
Q
departure from the parties’ agreed arbitral procedure, was a denial of due
R process, and hence in conflict with the public policy of Hong Kong, and
R
should be set aside.
S
S
T
T
U
U
V
V

- 11 -
A A
The applicable legal principles
B
B
25. Section 67 of the Ordinance applies Article 31 of the
C
Model Law. Article 31 (2) provides that an award shall state the reasons C
D upon which it is based, unless the parties have agreed that no reasons are to
D
be given.
E
E
F 26. As already pointed out in the preceding paragraphs of this
F
Decision, the grounds for setting aside and refusal of enforcement of an
G
G
award are to be construed narrowly, and it has to be shown by the applicant
H that the error complained of is egregious to warrant the setting aside of the
H
award.
I
I
J 27. As already emphasized in the decision of Z v R and the cases
J
referred to therein:
K
K
“18. The legal principles do not appear to be in real dispute. In
L determining whether the ground of the tribunal’s failure to deal
L
with all issues is established, there has to be an ‘issue’ which has
been put to the tribunal, and it has to be shown that the tribunal
M
failed to deal with the issue and that such failure has caused M
substantial injustice. Counsel for the Plaintiff relies on
Petrochemical Industries Co v Dow Chemical [2012]
N
N
EWHC 2739 (Comm). The Plaintiff accepts that the tribunal
does not have to set out each step by which it reaches its
O conclusion, and a tribunal’s decision on an issue without giving
O
reasons does not constitute failure to deal with it. Counsel relies
on the fact that it must be made apparent from the award that the
P
tribunal has addressed the issues which it has to revolve, P
referring to the observations made by the Court in Buyuk
Q Camlica Shipping Trading and Industry Co Inc v Progress Bulk
Q
Carriers Ltd [2010] EWHC 442 (Comm), at para 38, in the
context of an application made under section 68 of the
R
Arbitration Act 1996 to set aside an award on the ground of R
serious irregularity:
S
S
‘... there should be some form of communication, normally
in the form of a decision, by an arbitral tribunal to the
T parties from which the latter can ascertain whether or not
T
an essential issue has been dealt with. It is not sufficient for
U
U
V
V

- 12 -
A A
an arbitral tribunal to deal with crucial issues in pectore,
B
such that the parties are left to guess at whether a crucial B
issue has been dealt with or has been overlooked: the
legislative purpose of section 68 (2) (d) is to ensure that all
C
C
those issues the determination of which are crucial to the
tribunal’s decisions are dealt with and, in my judgment,
D this can only be achieved in practice if it is made apparent
D
to the parties (normally, as I say, from the Award or
Reasons) that those crucial issues have indeed been
E
determined.” (Emphasis added) E
F 19. In N v C [2019] HKCFI 2292, this Court emphasized that a
F
failure to deal with an issue is not equivalent to failure to deal
with an argument or a submission made or advanced at the
G hearing. The decision in Weldon Plant Ltd v The Commission
G
for the New Towns [2000] BLR 496 was referred to, where the
English court explained (again in the context of section 68 (2) (d)
H
of the Arbitration Act): H
I ‘It is concerned with a failure, that is to say where the
I
arbitral tribunal has not dealt at all with the case of a party
so that a substantial injustice has resulted, eg where a claim
J
has been overlooked, or where the decision cannot be J
justified as a particular key issue has not been decided
which is crucial to the result.’ (Emphases added)
K
K
20. As the Plaintiff also accepts, the fact that the arbitrator had
L not given adequate reasons for his award, or sufficiently clarified
L
that an issue fell away because of the findings which had been
made, is not tantamount to the arbitrator having failed to deal
M
with an issue. In Secretary of State for the Home Department M
v Raytheon Systems Ltd [2014] EWHC 4375 (TCC) (cited in
N paragraph 35 of N v C), the point was made clear:
N
‘If the tribunal has dealt with the issue in any way,
O Section 68 (2) (d) is inapplicable and that is the end of the
O
inquiry (Primera at paragraph 40-1); it does not matter for
the purposes of Section 68 (2) (d) that the tribunal has dealt
P
with it well, badly or indifferently. P
Q ...
Q
A failure to provide any or any sufficient reasons for the
R decision is not the same as failing to deal with an issue
R
(Fidelity Management v Myriad International [2005] 2
Lloyd’s Rep 508, paragraph 10, World Trade Corporation,
S
paragraph 19). A failure by a tribunal to set out each step S
by which to reach its conclusion or deal with each point
T made by a party is not a failure to deal with an issue that
T
U
U
V
V

- 13 -
A A
was put to it (Hussman v Al Ameen [2000] 2 Lloyd’s
B
Rep 83). B
...
C
C
A tribunal does not fail to deal with issues if it does not
D answer every question that qualifies as an “issue”. It can
D
“deal with” an issue where that issue does not arise in view
of its decision on the facts or its legal conclusions. A
E
tribunal may deal with an issue by so deciding a logically E
anterior point such that the other issue does not arise
(Petrochemical Industries at paragraph 27). If the tribunal
F
decides all those issues put to it that were essential to be F
dealt with for the tribunal to come fairly to its decision on
G the dispute or disputes between the parties, it will have
G
dealt with all the issues (Buyuk Camlica Shipping Trading
& Industry Co Inc v Progress Bulk Carriers Ltd [2010]
H
EWHC 442 (Comm), paragraph 30).’ (Emphasis added) H
21. Needless to say, as it is trite, the issue before this Court is
I
I
not whether the tribunal came to the right conclusion. Whether
the tribunal is right on its findings of facts and law, whether its
J decision is supported by evidence, whether the tribunal gave
J
sufficient reasons for the findings, and the quality of the
reasoning, are not matters for consideration. As the Hong Kong
K
Court repeatedly emphasized, an application made under section K
81 of the Arbitration Ordinance is not an appeal on the merits or
L on law. The court is not concerned with the substantive
L
correctness of an award, but only with the structural integrity of
the arbitral process and it is only when there is a serious or
M
egregious denial of due process that the court can interfere M
(Grand Pacific Holdings Ltd v Pacific China Holdings Ltd (in liq)
N (No 1) [2012] 4 HKLRD 1).”
N
O
28. It is also clear from the authorities, that in considering the O
P important question of whether a tribunal has dealt with an issue, the
P
approach is to read the award in a “reasonable and commercial way
Q
Q
expecting, as is usually the case, that there will be no substantial fault that
R can be found with it” (Zermalt Holdings SA v Nu-Life Upholstery Repairs
R
Ltd [1985] 2 EGLR 14 at p14F per Bingham J). It has to be borne in mind
S
S
that the parties to the arbitration to whom the award was issued were aware
T of and understood how the issues had been presented to and argued before
T
U
U
V
V

- 14 -
A A
the tribunal. Reading the award may involve taking account of the parties’
B
B
submissions, but the submissions made by the parties cannot dictate how
C the tribunal structures the disposal of the dispute referred to it. Although
C
awards often respond to the parties’ submissions, they should not be read
D
D
in a vacuum, and the question is whether, properly understood, the award
E
has dealt with an issue which is key to the tribunal’s decision on the E
dispute referred to it in the arbitration. The tribunal is only required, under
F
F
Article 31(2), to state the reasons upon which the award is based.
G
G
29. In the case of Petrochemical Industries Company (KSC) v The
H
H
Dow Chemical Company [2012] EW HC 2739 (Comm), the Court
I
highlighted the distinction between “issues” and “arguments” advanced or I
“points” made by the parties:
J
J
“A distinction is drawn in the authorities between, on the one
K hand ‘issues’ and, on the other hand, what are variously referred
K
to as (for example) ‘arguments’ advanced or ‘points’ made by
parties to an arbitration or ‘lines of reasoning’ or ‘steps’ in an
L
argument (see, for example, Hussman (Europe) Ltd v Al Ameen L
Development & Trade Co [2000] 2 Lloyd's Rep 83, 97 and
M Bulfracht (Cyprus) Ltd v Boneset Shipping Co Ltd (The
M
‘Pamphilos’) [2002] 2 Lloyd’s Rep 681, 686). These authorities
demonstrate a consistent concern to maintain the ‘high threshold’
N
that has been said to be required for establishing a serious N
irregularity (see Lesotho Highlands Development Authority
O v Impergilo SpA and ors [2005] UKHL 34 paragraph 28 and the
O
other judicial observations collected by Tomlinson J in AAB AG
v Hochtief Airport GMBH and anor [2006] EWHC 388
P
paragraph 63). The concern has sometimes been emphasised by P
references to ‘essential’ issues or ‘key’ issues or ‘crucial’ issues
(see respectively, for example, Ascot Commodities NV v Olam
Q
Q
International Ltd [2002] 2 Lloyd's Rep 277, 284; Weldon Plant
v Commission for New Towns [2001] 1 All ER 264, 279; and
R Buyuk Camlica Shipping Trading and Industry Co Ltd
R
v Progress Bulk Carriers Ltd [2010] EWHC 442 (Comm.)), but
the adjectives are not, I think, intended to import a definitional
S
gloss upon the statute but simply allude to the requirement that S
the serious irregularity result in substantial injustice: Fidelity
T Management SA v Myriad International Holdings BV [2005]
T
U
U
V
V

- 15 -
A A
EWHC 1193 at paragraph 10. They do not, to my mind, go
B
further in providing a useful test for applying section 68(2)(d). B
I do not attempt the impossible task of defining what is an ‘issue’,
C
but I reject three suggested yardsticks: First, in the Fidelity C
Management case (at paragraph 9) it was suggested that the
D answer to whether something is an issue ‘should normally be
D
obvious’ if it is considered whether what has not been dealt with
by a tribunal is ‘capable of being formulated as an essential issue
E
of the nature of what should be included in an agreed list of E
issues being prepared for the purpose of a case management
F conference’. I do not find this helpful: what is appropriate for a
F
case management list of issues varies enormously depending on
